original_abstract: 10 Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated tremendous capabilities in solving com- 11 plex tasks, from quantitative reasoning to understanding natural language. However, LLMs 12 sometimes suffer from confabulations (or hallucinations) which can result in them making plau- 13 sible but incorrect statements (Bang et al., 2023; Borji, 2023). This hinders the use of current 14 large models in scientific discovery. Here we introduce FunSearch (short for searching in the 15 function space), an evolutionary procedure based on pairing a pre-trained LLM with a system- 16 atic evaluator. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach to surpass the best known re- 17 sults in important problems, pushing the boundary of existing LLM-based approaches (Lehman 18 et al., 2022). Applying FunSearch to a central problem in extremal combinatorics — the cap 19 set problem — we discover new constructions of large cap sets going beyond the best known 20 ones, both in finite dimensional and asymptotic cases. This represents the first discoveries made 21 for established open problems using LLMs. We showcase the generality of FunSearch by apply- 22 ing it to an algorithmic problem, online bin packing, finding new heuristics that improve upon 23 widely used baselines. In contrast to most computer search approaches, FunSearch searches for 24 programs that describe how to solve a problem, rather than what the solution is. Beyond being 25 an effective and scalable strategy, discovered programs tend to be more interpretable than raw 26 solutions, enabling feedback loops between domain experts and FunSearch, and the deployment 27 of such programs in real-world applications. 28 Many problems in mathematical sciences are “easy to evaluate,” despite being typically “hard to 29 solve.” For example, in computer science, NP-complete optimization problems admit a polynomial- 30 time evaluation procedure (measuring the quality of the solution), despite the widespread belief that 31 no polynomia
humanized_abstract: 10 Large Language Models (LLMs) have been applied to solve a wide spectrum of difficult problems that range from basic quantitative abilities to high-level communication. These models suffer from confabulations (or hallucinations) that lead to producing reasonably-sounding yet false statements that have been denoted as hallucinations in the literature (Bang et al., 2023; Borji, 2023). Most current LLMs fail to conduct real scientific discovery. Here, we describe the first-ever FunSearch (short for searching in the function space) evolutionary procedure for coupled a LLM with a programmatic evaluator. We show by several demonstrations how FunSearch leads to advances on several record results for LLMs (Lehman et al., 2022). More importantly, using FunSearch we improved the state-of-the-art record in one of the most famous and basic extremal combinatorics (XTC) open problems, known as the cap set problem for finite dimensional sets (CSP); we also showed how new asymptotic cap set records can be computed. In other words, we provided the first-ever discoveries regarding an important, open problem, which were achieved using a Large Language Model; a feat that we are not aware has ever been done in the past for well established open problems, and that we demonstrate quantitatively here. We also showed in a completely different setting, namely algorithmic computer science, the capabilities of the approach by using FunSearch to improve many state-of-the-art heuristics for a well studied and practical bin packing problem which is also the classical problem underlying storage and packing in cloud object storage for Bin/Pack, which had never been properly approached in the optimization literature. FunSearch gives a method that for the first time leads to improvements and/or records on actual open problems and also beyond. All prior methods in computer search deal with searching and exploring solutions, meaning that they search for instances of the solutions in the solution space while FunSearch rather searches for the representations of the solutions in the function space; it finds a “how to solve this” rather than just a “what is the solution.” A functional representation is also considerably more human and natural to interpret for any given solution. An important property of the FunSearch procedure in this regard, is that it affords the possibility of utilizing human insights at two levels of the computational procedure that give an opportunity for true feedback loops between humans and the black/white box procedures provided by the computer searches; such feedback loops are also a prerequisite in order to achieve successful deployment of any final solutions found and suggested by FunSearch.
